title: Exploratory factor analysis on numeric data
summary: Rust port of psych::fa: iterative principal-axis / minimum-residual factor extraction with Kaiser-normalized analytic Varimax rotation. Iterates over meta batches and a range of n_factors values, emitting one xlsx workbook with per-combination summary + loadings sheets.
description: For each batch and each n_factors in [min_count, min(max_count, n_vars-1)]: builds the correlation matrix, extracts factors (MinRes/PA both route to iterative principal-axis with SMC communality initialisation), rotates loadings, and writes eigenvalues (all dims: value, diff, prop, cumulative), per-variable loadings MR1..MRk, communality h², uniqueness u², Hoffman complexity (Σl²)²/Σl⁴, and dominant factor index. Rows are sorted within each sheet by dominant factor ascending then |loading| descending — matching psych's `fa.sort`. Sheet names follow `<batch>_k<n>_sum` and `<batch>_k<n>_load`, truncated to Excel's 31-char limit.
usage:
[["factor-analysis::fa"]]
input = "./data.csv"
meta = "./meta.csv"
run_dir = "./runs"
run_name = "fa1"
min_count = 2
max_count = 5
cor = "pearson"
fm = "minres"
rotate = "varimax"

arguments.name: input
arguments.description: CSV of numeric responses; one row per observation, one column per variable.

arguments.name: meta
arguments.description: Variable metadata CSV with columns variable/label/batch/type. Missing label defaults to variable; missing type defaults to numeric.

arguments.name: run_name
arguments.description: Short identifier for this run; no '..', '/' or '\\'.

arguments.name: batches
arguments.description: Subset of batches to analyse. Empty = one FA per batch, or a single `all` batch if meta has no batch column.

arguments.name: min_count
arguments.description: Smallest n_factors to extract per batch.

arguments.name: max_count
arguments.description: Largest n_factors to extract per batch. Clamped to (n_vars - 1).

arguments.name: fm
arguments.description: Factoring method: minres|pa|uls|ols|wls|gls|ml|minchi|minrank|alpha. Currently minres and pa (iterative principal-axis); other values error at runtime pending Phase 3.

arguments.name: rotate
arguments.description: Rotation: none|varimax|quartimax|bentler_t|equamax|varimin|geomin_t|bifactor|promax|oblimin|simplimax|bentler_q|geomin_q|biquartimin|cluster. Currently none and varimax; other values error at runtime pending Phase 3.

arguments.name: correct
arguments.description: Continuity correction for tetrachoric/polychoric zero cells (matches psych defaults).

arguments.name: na_action
arguments.description: complete_cases (pairwise deletion) or fail (abort on NA).

arguments.name: headers
arguments.description: Whether CSV inputs have a header row.

arguments.name: threads
arguments.description: Number of worker threads for parallel batch × n_factors fan-out and per-pair correlation parallelism. `1` (default) runs serially. Results are deterministic regardless of thread count.

arguments.name: output_format
arguments.description: Override stdout format: human, protocol, or quiet.

notes: Current delivery: full correlation family (Phase 2), MinRes/PA extraction, Varimax rotation. Remaining fm/rotate values are accepted by the validator but return a NotImplemented runtime error until Phase 3.
Sample size for each batch is the minimum non-zero n_obs across all correlation cells — a conservative choice matching psych::fa's default pairwise behaviour.
The `com` column is Hoffman's complexity: 1.0 means a variable loads on one factor; values approach n_factors as loadings are spread evenly.

artifacts.title: Results workbook (one file, two sheets per group)
artifacts.description: Every item group produces two sheets: a summary sheet of eigenvalues and variance explained, and a loadings sheet of the per-item results. Both sheets stack the results for every factor count you requested (from `min_count` to `max_count`) in a single table so you can compare them side-by-side.

artifacts.title: Eigenvalue summary (one per item group)
artifacts.description: The diagnostic table for deciding how many factors to keep. Reading left to right: the metadata columns identify the run; the remaining columns give you one row per (n_factors, factor). Compare eigenvalues across factor counts to find the knee in the scree plot — the point after which additional factors add little variance.

fields.name: batch
fields.description: The item-group label (e.g. `attitudes`, `satisfaction`). Constant within the sheet.

fields.name: cor
fields.description: The correlation method used (e.g. `pearson`). Constant within the sheet.

fields.name: fm
fields.description: The factoring method used (e.g. `minres`). Constant within the sheet.

fields.name: rotate
fields.description: The rotation applied (e.g. `varimax`). Constant within the sheet.

fields.name: n_obs
fields.description: Sample size used — the smallest pairwise n across the correlation matrix for this group.

fields.name: n_factors
fields.description: How many factors were extracted for this block of rows. The sheet contains blocks for every value from `min_count` to `max_count`.

fields.name: iterations
fields.description: How many iterations the extraction took to converge. Very high counts (near the internal cap) can signal an unstable solution.

fields.name: converged
fields.description: `true` if the extraction converged cleanly. `false` means the solution may be unreliable — inspect the loadings before trusting it.

fields.name: dim
fields.description: The factor index within this block (1 = first factor, 2 = second factor, …). There are `n_factors` rows per block.

fields.name: eigenvalue
fields.description: How much of the total variance this factor captures, in raw units. Larger = more important. The classic Kaiser rule drops factors below 1.0.

fields.name: diff
fields.description: The gap between this eigenvalue and the next. A large drop followed by small drops is the 'elbow' of the scree plot.

fields.name: prop
fields.description: Proportion of the total variance explained by this factor (eigenvalue / total). A quick read on how much signal the factor carries.

fields.name: cumulative
fields.description: Running total of `prop` down the block. Useful for answering 'how much of the variance do my top k factors jointly explain?'.

artifacts.title: Factor loadings (one per item group)
artifacts.description: The interpretive table — this is where you decide what each factor means. Each row is one item within one factor-count solution. The MR columns hold the rotated loadings. Items are sorted so that everything loading on Factor 1 appears first (strongest first), then everything loading on Factor 2, and so on — the same ordering as R's `psych::fa.sort`.

fields.name: n_factors
fields.description: How many factors were extracted for this block. The sheet contains blocks for every value from `min_count` to `max_count`.

fields.name: variable
fields.description: The item's column name in the input data.

fields.name: label
fields.description: The human-readable label. Taken from the SPSS variable label, the `label` or `label_template` in meta, or the column name as a fallback.

fields.name: MR1 … MR<max_k>
fields.description: The rotated loading of this item on each factor. Values range roughly from −1 to 1. Loadings above 0.4 (or below −0.4) are generally strong enough to matter; loadings below 0.3 are typically ignored when naming a factor. Columns widen to the largest factor count in the sheet; rows for smaller counts leave higher-numbered MR columns blank.

fields.name: h2
fields.description: Communality — the share of this item's variance explained by all the factors together. Values from 0 to 1; higher is better. An item with h² below 0.3 isn't really part of the factor structure and may be a candidate for removal.

fields.name: u2
fields.description: Uniqueness — the share of this item's variance not explained by the factors (1 − h²). The item's 'personality', unrelated to the shared themes.

fields.name: com
fields.description: Hoffman's complexity. 1.0 means the item loads cleanly on one factor (the ideal); values near `n_factors` mean the item is spread evenly across every factor (the worst case). A tidy solution has most items near 1.0.

fields.name: dominant
fields.description: The index (1, 2, 3, …) of the factor this item loads on most strongly. Useful as a quick sort key and as the glue that holds the 'items on this factor' story together.

details:
For each combination of (item group, number of factors) the plugin runs a full factor-analysis pipeline: compute the correlation matrix with the chosen method, extract the requested number of factors, rotate the loading matrix to improve interpretability, and record the per-factor eigenvalues and per-item loadings. Within each loadings sheet, rows are sorted so that items on the same dominant factor appear together and the strongest loading appears first — the same ordering R’s `psych::fa.sort` produces.

Sample size is reported per item group as the smallest pairwise n across the correlation matrix. This is conservative — if you have a lot of missing data it will reflect the weakest overlap, not an average. Factor loadings are deterministic: the same input data and settings always produce the same numbers, even across thread counts.

A few common gotchas: tetrachoric correlations require binary items (two values) and polychoric correlations require ordered categorical items, so mark the `type` correctly in your meta. Items with zero variance (e.g. everyone answered the same value) are silently dropped. If you request more factors than the group can support, the max is clamped down automatically and a notice is emitted.

see_also.description: Build just the correlation matrix without running the factor extraction — handy for preliminary diagnostics or feeding into a downstream tool.
The mixed-data cousin. Use FAMD when your item group contains both numeric and categorical variables (e.g. attitudinal items plus demographic segments).

examples.title: CSV input with an external meta file
examples.body:
[["factor-analysis::fa"]]
input = "./data.csv"
meta = "./meta.csv"
run_dir = "./runs"
run_name = "fa1"
min_count = 2
max_count = 5
cor = "pearson"
rotate = "varimax"

examples.title: Dichotomous items with tetrachoric correlations
examples.body:
[["factor-analysis::fa"]]
input = "./items.sav"
run_dir = "./runs"
run_name = "tetra"
cor = "tetrachoric"
rotate = "varimax"

# type = dichotomous is required for tetrachoric correlations.
meta = [
  { match = "^P1_", batch = "attitudes", type = "dichotomous" },
]
